content.body:
Exothermic: releases energy (heat) to surroundings
→ Temperature RISES
→ ΔH is NEGATIVE
Examples: combustion, neutralisation, respiration, oxidation

Endothermic: absorbs energy from surroundings
→ Temperature FALLS
→ ΔH is POSITIVE
Examples: photosynthesis, thermal decomposition, dissolving ammonium salts, citric acid + sodium hydrogencarbonate

content.title: Energy Level Diagrams
content.body:
Exothermic: reactants at higher energy → products at lower energy. Energy difference released as heat. Activation energy is a 'hill' to start the reaction.

Endothermic: reactants lower energy → products higher energy. Energy absorbed from surroundings.

Activation energy: minimum energy needed to start a reaction

content.title: Everyday Examples
content.body:
Hand warmers: exothermic (iron oxidation)
Instant cold packs: endothermic (ammonium nitrate dissolving)
Combustion of fuels: exothermic
Cooking food: endothermic
Acid dissolving metal: exothermic

questions.question: Exothermic reaction releases heat. Temperature of surroundings will
questions.explanation: Exothermic = heat OUT to surroundings → surroundings get warmer

questions.question: ΔH is negative for
questions.explanation: Exothermic: energy released, ΔH < 0 (products at lower energy than reactants)

questions.question: Photosynthesis is endothermic because
options: it absorbs light energy to drive the reaction
questions.answer: it absorbs light energy to drive the reaction
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis stores light energy as chemical energy in glucose — energy is absorbed, not released

questions.question: Combustion is an example of
questions.explanation: Burning fuels releases large amounts of heat energy to surroundings

questions.question: Activation energy is
options: the energy released in a reaction
the minimum energy needed to start a reaction
questions.answer: the minimum energy needed to start a reaction
questions.explanation: Activation energy: threshold energy barrier that reactants must overcome to react

questions.question: In an energy level diagram for endothermic reaction, products are
questions.explanation: Endothermic: energy absorbed → products are at higher energy level than reactants

questions.question: Dissolving ammonium nitrate in water makes the water feel
questions.explanation: NH₄NO₃ dissolution is endothermic — absorbs heat from water, lowering temperature (cold packs)

questions.question: Neutralisation reactions are
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: acid + alkali → salt + water always releases heat (exothermic)

questions.question: Bond breaking is
questions.explanation: Breaking bonds REQUIRES energy (endothermic); forming bonds RELEASES energy (exothermic)

questions.question: Overall reaction is exothermic if
options: energy to break bonds > energy released forming bonds
energy to break bonds < energy released forming bonds
questions.answer: energy to break bonds < energy released forming bonds
questions.explanation: If bond forming releases more energy than bond breaking requires, net energy is released (exothermic)

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body:
Complete combustion (excess oxygen):
Hydrocarbon + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O + energy
CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O

Incomplete combustion (limited oxygen):
Produces CO (toxic) and C (soot) instead of CO₂
2CH₄ + 3O₂ → 2CO + 4H₂O

Energy from fuels:
ΔH_combustion measured by calorimetry
Q = mcΔT (heat transferred = mass × specific heat × temp change)

content.title: Fossil Fuels
content.body:
Coal: mainly carbon. Oldest fossil fuel. Most CO₂ per unit energy.
Petrol/Oil: mixture of hydrocarbons. Refined by fractional distillation.
Natural gas: mainly methane. Cleanest fossil fuel (most H relative to C).
All release CO₂ when burned → greenhouse effect
SO₂ from sulphur impurities → acid rain

content.title: Calorimetry
content.body:
Burning 1g of ethanol heats 200g water from 20°C to 46°C
Q = mcΔT = 200×4.18×26 = 21,736 J = 21.7 kJ
Molar mass of ethanol = 46g/mol
ΔH_c = 21.7 × 46 = -999 kJ/mol (compare to actual -1368 kJ/mol — heat loss in simple experiment)

questions.question: Complete combustion of methane produces
questions.explanation: Complete: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O (with excess oxygen)

questions.question: Carbon monoxide (CO) is produced in
questions.explanation: Incomplete combustion (limited O₂): carbon not fully oxidised → CO (toxic) instead of CO₂

questions.question: CO is dangerous because
options: it binds to haemoglobin preventing O₂ transport
questions.answer: it binds to haemoglobin preventing O₂ transport
questions.explanation: CO has 200× greater affinity for Hb than O₂ → carboxyhaemoglobin → oxygen deprivation → death

questions.question: Q = mcΔT. Water mass=100g, specific heat=4.18 J/gK, ΔT=20°C. Q =
Fossil fuels are non-renewable because
options: they took millions of years to form and are used faster than replaced
questions.answer: they took millions of years to form and are used faster than replaced
questions.explanation: Fossil fuels: formed over 300 million years. Current usage rate means they will run out in centuries

questions.question: Natural gas (methane) is the cleanest fossil fuel because
options: it has the highest H:C ratio producing least CO₂ per unit energy
questions.answer: it has the highest H:C ratio producing least CO₂ per unit energy
questions.explanation: Methane CH₄: H:C=4, produces 1CO₂ per CH₄. Higher H:C = more water, less CO₂ per unit energy

questions.question: SO₂ from burning coal contributes to
questions.explanation: Sulphur dioxide dissolves in rainwater forming sulphurous/sulphuric acid — damages buildings and ecosystems

questions.question: Fractional distillation of crude oil separates fractions based on
options: different boiling points of hydrocarbon fractions
questions.answer: different boiling points of hydrocarbon fractions
questions.explanation: Crude oil fractions have different chain lengths → different boiling points → separated by distillation

questions.question: Biofuels are considered carbon-neutral because
options: CO₂ released when burned equals CO₂ absorbed when crops grew
questions.answer: CO₂ released when burned equals CO₂ absorbed when crops grew
questions.explanation: Net CO₂ ≈ zero: crops absorb CO₂ growing; burning releases it back — approximately balanced cycle

questions.question: Calorimetry measures energy changes in reactions by
questions.explanation: Calorimeter: reaction heats water; Q=mcΔT gives heat transferred to/from water

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

lessons.title: Enthalpy Changes: Hess's Law and Bond Energies

content.body:
Hess's Law:
Enthalpy change of reaction is same regardless of route
ΔH_reaction = ΣΔH_formation(products) - ΣΔH_formation(reactants)

Bond energy method:
ΔH = Σ(bond energies broken) - Σ(bond energies formed)
(Breaking bonds: +energy; Forming bonds: -energy)

Standard conditions: 25°C (298K), 1 atm, 1 mol/L

content.title: Hess's Law Example
content.body:
Find ΔH for C + O₂ → CO₂ from:
C + O₂ → CO₂   ΔH = -394 kJ/mol (direct)... 
or via CO:
C + ½O₂ → CO   ΔH₁ = -111 kJ/mol
CO + ½O₂ → CO₂  ΔH₂ = -283 kJ/mol
Total: ΔH = -111 + (-283) = -394 kJ/mol ✓

content.title: Bond Energy Method
content.body:
CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O
Bonds broken: 4(C-H)=4×413=1652, 2(O=O)=2×498=996. Total=2648
Bonds formed: 2(C=O)=2×805=1610, 4(O-H)=4×463=1852. Total=3462
ΔH = 2648-3462 = -814 kJ/mol (experimental: -890 kJ/mol)

questions.question: Hess's Law states total enthalpy change is
questions.explanation: Hess's Law: ΔH is a state function — depends only on initial and final states, not on the path

questions.question: Bond breaking is
questions.explanation: Breaking bonds always requires energy input (endothermic). Bond energy = energy needed to break 1 mol of bonds

questions.question: Bond forming is
questions.explanation: Forming bonds releases energy — electrons move to lower energy when bonds form

questions.question: ΔH using bond energies =
questions.explanation: ΔH = Σ(energies of bonds broken) - Σ(energies of bonds formed)

questions.question: Standard enthalpy of formation ΔH°f is enthalpy change when
options: 1 mole of compound forms from elements in standard states
questions.answer: 1 mole of compound forms from elements in standard states
questions.explanation: ΔH°f: standard enthalpy of formation — enthalpy of forming 1 mol from elements (each in standard state) at 298K, 1 atm

questions.question: ΔH°f of an element in standard state (e.g. O₂, Fe) =
questions.explanation: By definition: elements in standard states are the reference → their formation enthalpy = 0

questions.question: Using Hess's: ΔH for A→C = ΔH(A→B) + ΔH(B→C). If ΔH(A→B)=-200, ΔH(B→C)=+80. ΔH(A→C)=
Average bond energy of C-H from combustion data is approximately
questions.explanation: C-H bond energy ≈ 413 kJ/mol (mean bond enthalpy from multiple compounds)

questions.question: Lattice enthalpy is the enthalpy change when
options: ionic compound dissociates into gaseous ions
questions.answer: ionic compound dissociates into gaseous ions
questions.explanation: Lattice enthalpy: energy to separate ionic crystal into gaseous ions — measure of ionic bond strength

questions.question: Born-Haber cycle is used to find
options: lattice enthalpy of ionic compounds using Hess's law
questions.answer: lattice enthalpy of ionic compounds using Hess's law
questions.explanation: Born-Haber cycle: Hess's law applied to ionic compound formation, allowing calculation of unmeasurable lattice enthalpy

questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

lessons.title: Rates of Reaction: Factors and Theories

content.body:
Rate of reaction = change in amount / time
Can measure: decrease in reactant or increase in product

Factors affecting rate:
1. Concentration: more particles per volume → more collisions
2. Temperature: more energy → more successful collisions
3. Surface area: more contact → more collisions
4. Catalyst: lowers activation energy Eₐ
5. Pressure (gases): more particles per volume

Collision theory: reaction occurs when particles collide with
sufficient energy (≥ activation energy) and correct orientation

content.title: Maxwell-Boltzmann Distribution
content.body:
Distribution of kinetic energies at temperature T
Area under curve = total number of molecules
Shaded area (E≥Eₐ) = fraction that can react
Higher T: curve broader, more molecules have E≥Eₐ
Catalyst: lowers Eₐ → larger shaded area → faster reaction

content.title: Measuring Rate
content.body:
Gas collected over water: measure volume vs time
Mass loss: measure on balance as gas escapes
Color change: colorimetry (e.g. iodine clock)
Conductivity change: ions formed/consumed
Initial rate from gradient of concentration-time graph

questions.question: Increasing concentration increases rate because
options: there are more molecules per unit volume → more frequent collisions
questions.answer: there are more molecules per unit volume → more frequent collisions
questions.explanation: Higher concentration: more particles in same volume → more collisions per second → faster rate

questions.question: Temperature increase doubles or triples rate approximately every 10°C because
options: more molecules exceed activation energy threshold AND average energy increases
questions.answer: more molecules exceed activation energy threshold AND average energy increases
questions.explanation: Temperature: not just more collisions but exponentially more molecules have energy ≥ Eₐ (Arrhenius equation)

questions.question: Catalyst increases rate by
questions.explanation: Catalyst: lowers Eₐ → more molecules have sufficient energy → faster rate. Catalyst regenerated at end

questions.question: Maxwell-Boltzmann curve: shaded area to right of Eₐ represents
options: molecules with sufficient energy to react
questions.answer: molecules with sufficient energy to react
questions.explanation: Area under M-B curve to right of activation energy = fraction of molecules that can react

questions.question: Marble chips + HCl: using powder instead of lumps increases rate because
options: smaller particles have greater total surface area → more contact for reaction
questions.answer: smaller particles have greater total surface area → more contact for reaction
questions.explanation: Smaller particles: same mass but more surface area exposed → more acid-marble collisions → faster rate

questions.question: Effect of catalyst on Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution
options: raises the Eₐ line to right
lowers the Eₐ line → more molecules have E≥Eₐ
questions.answer: lowers the Eₐ line → more molecules have E≥Eₐ
questions.explanation: Catalyst: Eₐ reduced. On M-B diagram: Eₐ line moves left → larger shaded area → faster rate

questions.question: Which does NOT affect rate for a reaction in solution?
questions.explanation: Inert gas pressure doesn't affect solution reactions — doesn't change reactant concentration or energy

questions.question: Iodine clock reaction is used to measure
options: time for reactant concentration to reach threshold (sudden colour change at endpoint)
questions.answer: time for reactant concentration to reach threshold (sudden colour change at endpoint)
questions.explanation: Iodine clock: reaction produces I₂ which instantly reacts with thiosulfate. When thiosulfate exhausted: sudden blue-black starch colour

questions.question: Correct orientation in collision theory means
options: molecules must hit at the right angle so reactive parts make contact
questions.answer: molecules must hit at the right angle so reactive parts make contact
questions.explanation: Even with sufficient energy, molecules in wrong orientation fail to react — bonds don't form properly

questions.question: Arrhenius equation k=Ae^(-Ea/RT) shows rate constant k
questions.explanation: k∝e^(-Ea/RT): as T increases, exponent less negative → k increases exponentially → rate increases

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Energy Changes. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Energy Changes concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of Energy Changes builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Energy Changes. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Energy Changes. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Energy Changes include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Energy Changes. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).

Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).
